class_schedule.topic: Why Educators Should Code: Computational Thinking and Beyond
learning_objectives: Define computational thinking and its four components
Explain why programming literacy matters for educational technologists
Set up a Python development environment
readings: Grover, S. & Pea, R. - Computational Thinking in K-12: A Review

lecture_notes.introduction: You are not becoming a software engineer. You are becoming an educational technologist who can speak the language of technology, automate repetitive tasks, build prototypes, evaluate technical claims, and understand the systems you specify and procure.
key_concepts: Computational thinking: decomposition, pattern recognition, abstraction, algorithm design
Why educators need programming: automation, data analysis, prototyping, technical literacy
Python: readable, versatile, widely used in education and data science
Development environment setup: VS Code, Python 3, terminal basics
Your first program: hello world and basic I/O
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setup and first program: install Python and VS Code, write a program that asks the user's name and grade level, then prints a personalized welcome message
setup_requirements: Computer with admin privileges for software installation

review.value: Not about coding per se, but about problem-solving using decomposition (break it down), pattern recognition (find similarities), abstraction (identify the essential), and algorithm design (create step-by-step solutions).
Python reads like English, has a gentle learning curve, has rich libraries for data analysis and web development, and is the most common language in data science and educational technology tools.

class_schedule.topic: Variables, Data Types, and Basic Operations
learning_objectives: Declare and use variables of different data types in Python
Write programs that process user input and produce formatted output
readings: Sweigart, A. - Automate the Boring Stuff with Python Ch. 1-2 (free online)

lecture_notes.introduction: Variables are containers for data. Understanding data types — numbers, text, booleans, lists — is the foundation for everything else in programming. Today we build that foundation.
key_concepts: Variables: naming conventions, assignment, reassignment
Data types: int, float, str, bool
Type conversion: int(), float(), str()
String operations: concatenation, f-strings, slicing
Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /, //, %, **
lecture_notes.demonstration: Grade calculator: write a program that takes three quiz scores, calculates the average, and prints a formatted report with the student's name, scores, and average

review.value: f'Hello, {name}! Your average is {average:.1f}%' — f-strings let you embed variables and expressions directly in strings with formatting control. Much cleaner than string concatenation.
In Python, / gives float division (7/2 = 3.5) and // gives integer division (7//2 = 3). This matters when calculating things like grade distribution buckets.

learning_objectives: Write if/elif/else statements to implement decision logic
Use comparison and logical operators to create complex conditions
Design programs that make decisions based on data

lecture_notes.introduction: Programs that make decisions are programs that do useful work. Conditionals let your code choose between different paths based on data — just like the branching scenarios you may design for learners.
key_concepts: if/elif/else syntax and indentation
Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
Logical operators: and, or, not
Nested conditionals and guard clauses
Common patterns: input validation, grade classification, eligibility checking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Grade classifier: write a program that takes a numerical score and outputs the letter grade using a standard scale, with input validation to handle invalid inputs

review.value: Python uses indentation (4 spaces) to define code blocks. This is not cosmetic — incorrect indentation causes errors. This enforces readable code.
Always validate user input before using it. If the user enters 'abc' when you expect a number, your program will crash unless you check first. try/except blocks handle this gracefully.

learning_objectives: Write for and while loops to repeat operations on data
Use loops to process lists and files
Avoid common loop errors: infinite loops, off-by-one errors

lecture_notes.introduction: Loops let you process thousands of records with a few lines of code. For educators, this means automating tasks like grading calculations, data cleaning, and report generation that would take hours manually.
key_concepts: for loops: iterating over lists, ranges, and strings
while loops: repeating until a condition changes
List operations: creation, indexing, slicing, append, len()
Processing collections: calculating averages, finding min/max, counting
Breaking and continuing: early exit and skip patterns
lecture_notes.demonstration: Class roster processor: write a program that reads a list of student names and scores, calculates the class average, identifies the highest and lowest scores, and counts how many students scored above 80%

review.value: Use for when you know how many iterations (process each student). Use while when you do not know how many iterations (keep asking for input until the user types 'quit').
Python shorthand for creating lists from loops: passing_students = [s for s in students if s['score'] >= 70]. More concise and Pythonic than a multi-line for loop.

class_schedule.topic: Working with Data: Files, CSV, and JSON
learning_objectives: Read and write text files, CSV files, and JSON files in Python
Process educational data from real-world file formats
Clean and transform data for analysis
readings: Python csv and json module documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational data lives in files: grade exports from the LMS, survey results in CSV, course content in JSON, reports in text. Being able to read, process, and write these files programmatically is one of the most immediately useful programming skills for an educational technologist.
key_concepts: Reading and writing text files: open(), read(), write(), with statement
CSV processing: csv.reader, csv.DictReader, writing CSV output
JSON processing: json.load(), json.dumps(), nested data structures
Data cleaning: handling missing values, stripping whitespace, type conversion
Real-world application: processing LMS grade exports
lecture_notes.demonstration: LMS grade processor: write a program that reads a CSV export of LMS grades, calculates weighted averages for each student, flags students below 70%, and writes a summary report to a new CSV file
setup_requirements: Sample LMS grade export CSV file

review.value: csv.DictReader reads CSV files into dictionaries keyed by column headers. This means you can access data by column name (row['score']) instead of column index (row[3]), making code much more readable.

JSON is the lingua franca of web APIs and educational data. Course catalogs, LTI configurations, xAPI statements, and many LMS data exports use JSON. Being able to read and write JSON programmatically is immediately useful.

learning_objectives: Use Python to make API requests and process responses
Automate repetitive tasks with Python scripts
Understand the basics of web scraping for educational data collection

lecture_notes.introduction: The most practical programming skill for an educational technologist may be automation: writing scripts that do in seconds what would take you hours. API access lets you pull data from LMS platforms, and web scraping lets you collect data from public sources.
key_concepts: HTTP basics: GET, POST, status codes, headers
The requests library: making API calls in Python
JSON API responses: parsing and extracting data
LMS API examples: Canvas API for course data, grades, and enrollment
Automation scripts: file renaming, report generation, data merging, email sending
lecture_notes.demonstration: API data retrieval: write a Python script that calls a public educational data API (e.g., NCES or Open Library), processes the JSON response, and formats the results into a readable report
setup_requirements: Python environment with requests library installed
API documentation for a public educational data source

review.value: With the Canvas API and Python requests library, you can: pull all student grades for a course in one API call, identify students missing assignments, and generate a customized report — tasks that would take 30 minutes manually, done in 2 seconds.
If you do a task more than three times, automate it. Educational technologists who can write simple automation scripts save their organizations hundreds of hours per year.

questions.question: Write a Python function called classify_score that takes a numerical score (0-100) as input and returns the letter grade: A (90-100), B (80-89), C (70-79), D (60-69), F (below 60). Include input validation to handle scores outside 0-100.

rubric.key: Correct conditional logic with proper thresholds
Clean code with proper Python syntax

questions.question: What is the difference between = and == in Python? Give an example of when using the wrong one would cause a bug in an educational application.
questions.answer: = is the assignment operator (sets a variable to a value). == is the comparison operator (checks if two values are equal). Bug example: in a grade processing program, writing 'if student_grade = 'A':' instead of 'if student_grade == 'A':' would cause a SyntaxError in Python (though in some languages like C, it would silently assign 'A' to student_grade and always evaluate as true, giving every student an A). Even in Python where this is caught, confusing the two operators is a common beginner mistake that wastes debugging time.

rubric.key: Provides a specific educational application bug example
Explains the consequence of the error

questions.question: Write Python code that reads a list of student scores [85, 72, 90, 68, 95, 78, 82, 61, 88, 74] and prints: (1) the class average, (2) the number of students scoring 80 or above, and (3) the highest and lowest scores.
questions.answer:
scores = [85, 72, 90, 68, 95, 78, 82, 61, 88, 74]
average = sum(scores) / len(scores)
above_80 = len([s for s in scores if s >= 80])
highest = max(scores)
lowest = min(scores)
print(f'Class average: {average:.1f}')
print(f'Students scoring 80+: {above_80}')
print(f'Highest: {highest}, Lowest: {lowest}')

rubric.key: Correct identification of highest and lowest scores

questions.question: An educational technologist needs to process 500 student records from a CSV file exported from Canvas. Explain why Python is a better tool for this task than a spreadsheet, and describe the general approach (in pseudocode or plain English) for reading and processing the CSV.
questions.answer: Python is better than a spreadsheet for this task because: (1) Reproducibility — a Python script can be run repeatedly with different data files and always produces the same results. A spreadsheet formula sequence is harder to replicate and verify. (2) Automation — the script can be scheduled to run automatically whenever new data is exported. (3) Scalability — the same script works for 500 or 50,000 records without modification. (4) Error handling — Python can handle missing data, format inconsistencies, and edge cases programmatically. General approach: (1) Open the CSV file using csv.DictReader. (2) Loop through each row. (3) For each student, extract relevant fields (name, scores, submission dates). (4) Calculate derived values (weighted average, missing assignments, risk flags). (5) Write results to a new CSV file or generate a formatted report.

rubric.key: Identifies at least two advantages of Python over spreadsheets
Describes a correct general approach for CSV processing

major_assignments.title: Educational Data Processing Tool
major_assignments.description: Build a Python tool that solves a real data processing problem for educators. The tool must: (1) read data from a CSV or JSON file, (2) process the data using loops, conditionals, and functions, (3) produce a useful output (summary report, cleaned data file, or visualization), and (4) include error handling for common data quality issues. Submit the working code, sample data, output, and a documentation file explaining what the tool does and how to use it.
objectives: Apply Python programming skills to a real educational data problem
Build a functional tool that automates a manual process
Write clean, well-documented code with error handling
Produce user documentation for a non-technical audience
deliverables: Sample input data file (CSV or JSON)
Sample output produced by the tool
User documentation (1 page) explaining purpose, requirements, and usage
Brief code walkthrough document (1 page) explaining the logic

criteria: Tool runs without errors on the sample data
Handles edge cases and data quality issues
Code is well-organized with functions for distinct tasks
Variable names are descriptive and consistent
Comments explain the why, not just the what
Solves a genuine problem an educator would face
Saves significant time compared to manual processing
Output is formatted for practical use
User documentation is clear enough for a non-programmer to follow
Code walkthrough explains the logic and design decisions
Functional tool that solves a real educational data problem
Clear documentation for both technical and non-technical audiences

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly coding quizzes testing Python fundamentals

lab_assignments.description: Weekly coding exercises: progressively more complex Python programming tasks

midterm_examination.description: Practical coding exam: write Python programs to solve educational data problems

final_project.description: Extended automation project: build a multi-step data pipeline or web-based tool for an educational context

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Programming skills are built through practice with instructor support. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late coding assignments lose 5% per day for up to 5 days. Code must run without errors to receive credit.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussing approaches is encouraged. Sharing code is not permitted. If you get help from a classmate or online resource, cite it in your comments.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own. Copying code from the internet, classmates, or AI tools without attribution and understanding is a violation. Using AI tools to generate code must be disclosed and you must be able to explain every line.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with Python 3.x and VS Code installed. Internet access for library installation and API exercises.
